1: “But now, Job, hear my words.” Elihu addresses Job directly for the first time after his long preamble in Job 32. The wᵉʾûlām (but now) marks the transition from introduction to argument — Elihu has established his right to speak; now he will speak to the one the Friends could not answer. The invitation to šimʿ (hear/listen) mirrors the wisdom-instruction formula of Proverbs (e.g., Proverbs 4:1) — Elihu frames himself as a teacher, not a prosecutor.
2: “I have opened my mouth; my tongue speaks within my mouth.” The doubled description of speech-preparation — pāṯaḥtî pî (I have opened my mouth) and dibbᵉrāh lᵉšônî bᵉḥikkî (my tongue speaks within my palate) — is ceremonial, like a herald announcing official speech. Elihu is young; the formality is deliberate self-presentation. The MKT preserves both phrases rather than collapsing them, because the redundancy is itself meaningful: Elihu is performing the act of authoritative address.
3: “My words come from an upright heart; my lips speak what I know sincerely.” Yōšer-libbî (uprightness of my heart) and ḏaʿaṯ śᵉpātay bārûr (pure knowledge my lips speak) — Elihu claims both moral and epistemic integrity. The term bārûr (pure, clear) is a metallurgical term: smelted free of impurity. He distinguishes his speech from the Friends’ by asserting that he has not composed arguments to fit a position; the MKT’s “sincerely” captures the bārûr quality without the metallurgical image, which English would overload.
4: “The Spirit of God has made me, and the breath of the Almighty gives me life.” Rûaḥ-ʾēl (Spirit of God) and nišmaṯ šaddai (breath of the Almighty) are paired as the source of Elihu’s life — an echo of Genesis 2:7 (nišmaṯ ḥayyîm) and Job 12:10. Elihu grounds his authority not in age (like the Friends) but in the same divine breath that gave life to Adam. This prepares his argument that his speech participates in what God says through creatures. The MKT renders both nouns distinctly (Spirit/breath) to preserve the parallelism.
5: “If you can answer me, set your words in order before me and take your stand.” ʿᵃrāḵāh (set in order, marshal) is a legal-military term — the same root as “draw up in battle array” (cf. Job 6:4). Elihu invites Job to the same forensic contest Job has demanded of God throughout the dialogue (Job 9:14-15; Job 13:18; Job 23:4). The difference is that Elihu is a human being, not God — Job has no reason to be afraid of him (v7). The irony is that Elihu is attempting to do what Job’s môkîaḥ (umpire, Job 9:33) was supposed to do.
6: “I too was formed from clay.” Qōreṣtî (I was pinched off, formed) from ḥōmer (clay) — the same word-pair as Job 10:9 (“You formed me like clay”). Elihu claims creaturely solidarity with Job: both are clay-formed; neither can claim intrinsic advantage before God. The phrase gam-ʾᵃnî kᵉpiḵā (I too am like you) is a deliberate leveling gesture, undermining the Friends’ implicit superiority. The MKT “formed from clay” preserves both the humility claim and the creation-theology subtext.
7: “My dread will not terrify you, nor will my pressure be heavy upon you.” Elihu explicitly contrasts himself with God: Job has complained that God’s ʾêmāh (terror/dread) makes a fair hearing impossible (Job 9:34; Job 13:21). The ʾakkᵉḵā (my pressure upon you) uses the same weight-imagery as Job 23:2 (ʾânāḵāh — my complaint is heavier than groaning). Elihu is a human mediator who can engage without the ontological terror that makes Job’s encounter with God so fraught. He is, in effect, claiming to be the safe interlocutor Job longed for.
8: “You have spoken in my hearing.” Bᵉʾoznay (in my ears) — Elihu claims to have heard all of Job’s speeches firsthand. Vv8-11 are his summary of what Job said: a three-fold claim of innocence followed by a claim that God is Job’s enemy. The accuracy of the summary is significant: unlike the Friends who distorted Job’s words (e.g., Eliphaz in Job 22:6-9), Elihu quotes Job with reasonable precision. This is part of Elihu’s rhetorical self-presentation as a fair witness.
9: “You said: ‘I am clean, without transgression; I am innocent and there is no iniquity in me.’” Zak (pure, clean) and ḥattāʾṯ (transgression/sin) — Elihu’s summary of Job’s self-defense. Job has indeed claimed moral integrity throughout (Job 6:30; Job 9:21; Job 23:10; Job 27:5-6). The key term ḥāpēš pešāʿ (without transgression) uses pešaʿ, the term for deliberate, willful rebellion — not accidental sin. Job’s claim is strong: he has not deliberately rejected God. Elihu will not contest this directly but will reframe the question.
10: “But you also said: ‘He finds grievances against me; he regards me as his enemy.’” Tᵉnûʾôṯ (grievances, occasions against me) — the term suggests manufactured pretexts: God is not responding to real evidence but inventing reasons for hostility. ʾôyēḇ (enemy) is Job’s sharpest charge against God (Job 13:24; Job 16:9; Job 19:11). Elihu is accurate that this is what Job said. His upcoming argument will not deny that Job suffers, but will contest the interpretation: God-as-enemy is wrong; God speaks through suffering (for Job, not against him).
11: “He puts my feet in stocks; he watches all my paths.” Sāḏ (stocks/fetters) from Job 13:27 — Job’s complaint that God treats him as a prisoner under surveillance. Yišmōr (watches) carries the double sense of guarding/protecting and monitoring/spying; Job experiences God’s watch as hostile (ṣāpan lî, “watching over me for evil,” Job 10:14). Elihu is re-quoting Job’s words to set up the correction: the divine watch is not punitive surveillance but providential shepherding of a creature toward its own good.
12: “But in this I tell you plainly that you are wrong: God is greater than any human being.” Lōʾ-ṣāḏaqtā (you are not right/you are wrong) — Elihu’s direct verdict on Job’s position. The reason given is yirbaʿ ʾĕlôah mimmenneš (God is greater than any human being): God’s transcendence means the creature cannot set the terms of the relationship. This is partially right — the whirlwind speeches will make the same point (Job 38:4). But Elihu’s version lacks the personal engagement God brings; he argues from category rather than relationship.
13: “Why do you argue against him? For he does not give account for any of his actions.” Lōʾ-yᵃʿᵃneh ʿal-kol-dᵉḇārāyw (he does not answer for all his actions) — the divine non-accountability argument. This is Elihu’s strongest argument and his weakest: it is strong because it is true (God does not owe answers to creatures) but weak because it does not address why Job should trust a God who acts unaccountably. Elihu will partly answer this in vv14-30 by showing that God does communicate — through dreams and suffering.
14: “For God speaks in one way and then another, though people do not notice.” Bᵉʾaḥaṯ (in one way) and bišᵉtayim (in two/another) — the MKT’s “in one way and then another” captures the numerical idiom. The critical term is lōʾ yᵉšûrennāh (people do not perceive/notice it): the problem is not that God is silent but that human perception is inadequate. This reframes Job’s complaint from divine absence to human obtuseness — a partial but real answer to Job’s cry that God “hides his face” (Job 13:24).
15: “In a dream, in a nighttime vision, when deep sleep falls on people as they slumber on their beds.” Bᵉḥēzôn laylāh (nighttime vision) and tardēmāh (deep sleep) — the term tardēmāh is significant: it is the same word used for Adam’s deep sleep in Genesis 2:21 and Abram’s in Genesis 15:12 (where God cuts the covenant). The nocturnal divine communication channel Elihu describes is not peripheral but central to OT covenant experience. Job has received no such visions; his nights are torment (Job 7:14). Elihu’s point: God uses this channel; Job should attend to it.
16: “He opens people’s ears and confirms what he is teaching them.” Yigleh ʾōzen (opens/uncovers the ear) — the idiom means to whisper into someone’s ear; more literally, to remove the covering so hearing is possible. God is not broadcasting into static; he is preparing the ear. Wᵉbᵉmōsārām yaḥtōm (and in their correction he seals) — the “seals” here means confirms/impresses: God does not just communicate but ensures the message is received. This is the theology of divine perseverance in communication that Elihu presents against Job’s experience of silence.
17: “To turn them from their intended course and shield them from pride.” Lᵉhāsîr ʾāḏām mᵃʿᵃśeh (to turn a man from his deed/course) and gēwāh (pride) — the purpose of divine night-communication is redirective: away from a self-destructive course, away from pride. This echoes the wisdom literature’s consistent association of gēwāh/gāʾôn (pride) with the path to šaḥaṯ (destruction/pit). God disciplines not to punish but to redirect before the person reaches the edge. This is Elihu’s most generous interpretation of suffering.
18: “He keeps him from the pit and his life from crossing into death.” Šaḥaṯ (the pit) — Elihu’s first use of his key term; he will use it six more times in vv22-30. The pit is not just a metaphor for death but the place of permanent darkness below; šelaḥ (sword/weapon; or canal/channel) in some traditions is the name of a river crossing into death. The MKT “crossing into death” captures the spatial imagery: a threshold not to cross. God’s nocturnal discipline is pre-emptive preservation — keeping the person from arriving at the pit.
19: “He is also chastened with pain upon his bed, and with unending conflict in his bones.” Yûkāḥ (he is chastened/disciplined) from yākaḥ — the same root as Job’s longed-for môkîaḥ (umpire, Job 9:33). The suffering Elihu describes is disciplinary: kᵉʾēḇ (pain) on the bed and rîb ʿᵃṣāmāyw (strife/conflict of his bones) — inward physical suffering. Elihu’s framework exactly describes Job’s condition: Job is on his bed, in bone-pain. The implication — which Job resists — is that Job’s suffering is of this disciplinary type.
20: “So that he cannot eat bread and his appetite revolts against any food.” Zihhᵃmāṯtᵉhû (it loathes) describes nausea that makes even the smell of food repulsive. This is a clinical description of severe illness: appetite failure is among the last symptoms before death in ancient understanding (cf. Job 6:7). Elihu is particularizing the suffering: not vague distress but the specific loss of appetite that marks near-fatal illness. Job’s body has done exactly this (Job 3:20-22; Job 7:5). The purpose-clause framing (“so that” — this illness serves the goal of vv17-18) is Elihu’s interpretive move.
21: “His flesh wastes away until it is gone, and his bones that were hidden now protrude.” Kālāh ḇāśār (his flesh wastes/finishes) and šupî ʿᵃṣāmôṯāyw (his bones that were hidden/unseen) — the progression from appetite-loss to visible skeletal wasting. The bones “that were not seen” now protrude: this is the precise symptom Job displays in Job 19:20 (“my bones cling to my skin and to my flesh”). Elihu’s medical description applies to Job in real time while he speaks. The listener is meant to recognize: this is what is happening to Job, and it has redemptive purpose.
22: “His life draws near the pit and his soul to the agents of death.” Šaḥaṯ (the pit) appears again; mᵉmîtîm (agents of death) — possibly “those who kill” or death-messengers (LXX: “in death”). This is the nadir of the suffering arc Elihu describes: the person has arrived at the brink of the pit. But the very extremity sets up the intervention of v23. Elihu structures his theodicy as a drama: suffering serves a purpose up to the very edge of the pit; then God intervenes through the mediator. Job is at exactly this edge.
23: “But if there is a messenger for him, a mediator — one in a thousand.” Meliṣ (interpreter/mediator/messenger) is the pivotal term in Job 33 and among the most significant in the OT wisdom tradition. The root lûṣ means to interpret, translate, or intercede between parties. ʾeḥāḏ minnî-ʾālep (one from a thousand) conveys extreme rarity — not impossible but profoundly uncommon. This figure lᵉhaggîḏ lāʾāḏām yōšrô (declares to the person what is right for him) — functions exactly as Job’s môkîaḥ (Job 9:33) and gōʾēl (Job 19:25): a being who mediates between the dying person and God to declare what is actually the person’s yōšer (uprightness/right path). Unlike Job’s mediator-hope, Elihu’s meliṣ appears to already exist within God’s provision — the question is whether the sufferer has one.
24: “Then God is gracious to him and says: Spare him from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom.” Wayyeḥunnenû (and he is gracious to him) — divine ḥēn (grace) is the operative force. God’s verdict is pᵉḏêhû mēreḏeṯ šaḥaṯ (spare/ransom him from descending to the pit). Kōper (ransom) is the most loaded term in the verse: in cultic law, the kōper is the substitutionary payment that covers a liability (Exodus 30:12; Numbers 35:31-32). Here God himself māṣāʾtî kōper (I have found a ransom) — the ransom is not provided by the human being but discovered/provided by God. The structure anticipates the NT: the ransom for human life before the pit is not earned but given. The MKT “I have found a ransom” preserves the first-person divine speech embedded here.
25: “His flesh becomes fresher than a child’s; he is restored to the days of his youth.” Raṭap (fresh, supple) — used only here in the OT; the root suggests glistening moisture. Yāšôb lîmê ʿᵃlûmāyw (he returns to the days of his youth) — the restoration is not merely physical survival but renewal to the vitality of youth. This is hyperbolic but theologically serious: God’s ransom restores to a state prior to the destruction. In the book’s narrative arc, Job himself will experience this restoration (Job 42:10-17). Elihu’s theology of the pit-ransom is, in structure, correct — even if his application to Job misreads Job’s specific situation.
26: “He prays to God and God accepts him; he sees his face with a shout of joy, and God restores his righteousness.” Yiṣʿaq (he cries out/prays) and yirṣēhû (God accepts him) — the sequence: prayer → acceptance → face-vision → righteousness restored. Yirāʾeh pānāyw (he sees God’s face) is the language of restored relationship and throne-room access (cf. Psalms 17:15; Job 11:7). Wayyāšeḇ lᵉʾenôš ṣiḏqāṯô (he restores to the person his righteousness) — God gives back what suffering temporarily took. This is the full restoration arc: ransom (v24) → physical renewal (v25) → restored communion and righteousness (v26).
27: “He looks on people, and if any say: ‘I sinned and bent what was right, and it brought me nothing good.’” Yāšōr ʿal-ʾᵃnāšîm (he looks on/surveys people) — the restored person becomes a witness-testimony to others. The confession cited — ḥāṭāʾtî wᵉyāšār hiʿwêṯî (I sinned and perverted what was right) — is exactly what the Friends demanded of Job throughout the dialogue. But note the sequence here: the confession comes after the ransom and restoration, not as the precondition. The confession is the fruit of deliverance, not its cause. This subtly subverts the Friends’ theology while using their vocabulary.
28: “God will deliver his life from the pit, and his life will see the light.” Pāḏāh napšô mᵉʿᵃḇōr baššaḥaṯ (redeemed his life from crossing into the pit) — pāḏāh (redeem, ransom) is closely related to kōper in v24; both words denote the act of paying the price that releases someone from a claim. Ḥayyāyw bāʾôr tirʾeh (his life will see the light) — the light-versus-pit contrast that runs through the whole speech reaches its climax: the person who was at the edge of the pit now stands in the light of life. The ôr ḥayyîm (light of the living, v30) is the antithesis of the pit-darkness.
29: “See, God does all these things — again and again — with a person.” Kol-ʾēlleh yiṣʿ-ʾēl pᵉʿāmayim šālôš (two times, three times) — the graded numerical formula (twice/thrice) is the biblical idiom for “repeatedly, persistently.” God is not a single-intervention deity; he does the dream-warning, suffering-chastening, mediator-ransom cycle not once but as many times as needed. This responds to Job’s implicit complaint that God has abandoned him: Elihu insists God perseveres in reaching the human creature. The ʿim-gāḇer (with a man/person) keeps the register human and relational rather than cosmological.
30: “To bring his life back from the pit, that he might be lit by the light of the living.” Lᵉhāšîḇ napšô (to bring back his life/soul) and ʾôr bᵉʾôr haḥayyîm (light, in the light of the living) — the repetition of ʾôr (light) is emphatic. The purpose of everything Elihu has described — dreams, suffering, mediator, ransom — is this: that the human being lives in the light of the living rather than descending into the pit. This is Elihu’s theology of providence: every divine act toward the creature is ultimately directional — toward life, light, face-to-face restoration. It is the right theology; whether it applies to Job’s situation in the way Elihu implies is another question.
31: “Pay close attention, Job; listen to me; be silent and I will speak.” Elihu’s rhetorical instruction to Job after the theological display of vv14-30. Qᵉšāḇ-lî Iyyôḇ (pay attention to me, Job) — direct address. The double imperative šimʿ-lî hašqeṯ wᵉʾānōḵî ʾᵃḏabbēr (listen, be silent, and I will speak) mirrors the structure of teacher-to-student in wisdom literature. Elihu is trying to get Job into the posture of a learner, the posture the Friends’ bullying never achieved. The invitation is genuine; whether Job can receive it is the question the narrative leaves open.
32: “If you have anything to say, answer me; speak up, for I want to see you vindicated.” Ḥāpaṣtî ṣidqeḵā (I desire/want your vindication/righteousness) — Elihu makes an extraordinary claim: he is on Job’s side. He wants Job to be vindicated, not condemned. This is different from the Friends, who want Job to confess guilt so the theodicy holds. Elihu believes Job’s integrity is real (Job 32:3 — the Friends were wrong to condemn Job) but that Job’s interpretation of his situation is wrong. He is attempting to do what the môkîaḥ (umpire) would do: hold the space for both God’s righteousness and Job’s vindication.
33: “If not, listen to me; be silent, and I will teach you wisdom.” ḥoḵmāh (wisdom) — the speech ends with Elihu’s stated goal: wisdom, not Job’s defeat. The conditional structure (if you answer / if you don’t) offers Job genuine agency. The final word ḥoḵmāh is the book’s supreme value, last invoked in Job 28:28 (the fear of the Lord is wisdom; departure from evil is understanding). Elihu is positioning his speech as wisdom-teaching rather than prosecution. Whether he delivers on this claim is the interpretive challenge of Job 33-37.
